CHEMISTRY (Theory)

Time allowed:3 hours
Maximum Marks:70

General Instructions :

  1. All questions are compulsory.
  2. Section A : Questions number 1 to 5 are very short answer questions and carry 1 mark each.
  3. Section B : Questions number 6 to 12 are short answer questions and carry 2 marks each.
  4. Section C : Questions number 13 to 24 are also short answer questions and carry 3 marks each.
  5. Section D : Questions number 25 to 27 are long answer questions and carry 5 marks each.
  6. There is no overall choice. However, an internal choice has been provided in two questions of one mark, two questions of two marks, four questions of three marks and all the three questions of five marks weightage. You have to attempt only one of the choices in such questions.
  7. Use of log tables, if necessary. Use of calculators is not allowed.

SECTION A
1.

Out of Chlorobenzene and Cyclohexyl chloride, which one is more reactive towards nucleophilic substitution reaction and why?

Solution:

Cyclohexyl chloride; Because of partial double bond character of C-Cl bond in Chlorobenzene, Resonance effect and sp3 hybridised carbon in cyclohexyl chloride whereas sp2 carbon in chlorobenzene.

 1
2.

Arrange the following in decreasing order of solubility in water:
(C2H5)2NH, C2H5NH2, C6H5NH2

Solution:

C2H5NH2 > (C2H5)2NH > C6H5NH2

 1

Solution:

Chapter deleted from the textbook.

Solution:

Chapter deleted from the textbook.

 1

OR

Why does ZnO appear yellow on heating?

Solution:

ZnO is white at room temperature. ZnO loses oxygen on heating. The excess of Zn2+ ions moves to interstitial sites and vacant sites of anions are occupied by electrons which absorb light from visible region and radiate complementary colour i.e yellow.

{tex}ZnO\xrightarrow\triangle\;Zn^{2+}\;+\frac12O_2\;+2e^-{/tex}

 1
5.

What is the difference between amylose and amylopectin?

Solution:

Amylose is water soluble linear polymer of {tex}\alpha - {/tex}glucose whereas amylopectin is water insoluble branched chain polymer of {tex}\alpha - {/tex}glucose.

 1

OR

Write the products obtained after hydrolysis of lactose.

Solution:

Hydrolysis of Lactose gives D-galactose and D-glucose.

 1

SECTION B
6.

Define order of reaction. Predict the order of reaction in the given graphs:




  2. where [R]o is the initial concentration of reactant and t1/2 is half-life.
Solution:

Order of reaction: The order of reaction is defined as the sum of the powers of the concentration of the reactants in the rate equation.

  1. First order
  2. zero order
 2
7.

Give reasons:

  1. An increase in temperature is observed on mixing chloroform and acetone.
  2. Aquatic animals are more comfortable in cold water than in warm water.
Solution:
  1. Due to stronger interaction between chloroform and acetone than pure chloroform or acetone interactions.
  2. Because of high solubility of oxygen gas now KH value in cold water than in warm water.
 2
8.

When FeCr2O4 is fused with Na2CO3 in the presence of air it gives a yellow solution of compound (A). Compound (A) on acidification gives compound (B). Compound (B) on reaction with KCl forms an orange coloured compound (C). An acidified solution of compound (C) oxidises Na2SO3 to (D). Identify (A), (B), (C) and (D).

Solution:

FeCr2O4 is fused with Na2CO3 in the presence of air it gives a yellow solution of compound (A).

Compound (A) on acidification gives compound (B).

Compound (B) on reaction with KCl forms an orange coloured compound (C).

An acidified solution of compound (C) oxidises Na2SO3 to (D).


Therefore,
A = Na2CrO4 = Sodium chromate,
B= Na2Cr2O7 = Sodium dichromate,
C= K2Cr2O7 = Potassium dichromate,
D= Na2SO4 = Sodium sulfate

 2
9.

Write balanced chemical equations for the following processes:

  1. Cl2 is passed through slaked lime.
  2. SO2 gas is passed through an aqueous solution of Fe(III) salt.
Solution:
  1. {tex}2Ca(OH)_2 + 2Cl_2 \rightarrow CaCl_2 + Ca(OCl)_2 + 2H_2O{/tex}
  2. {tex}SO_2 + 2Fe^{3+} + 2H_2O  \rightarrow 2Fe^{2+} + SO_4^{2-} + 4H^+{/tex}
 2

OR
  1. Write two poisonous gases prepared from chlorine gas.
  2. Why does Cu2+ solution give blue colour on reaction with ammonia?
Solution:
  1. Mustard gas, tear gas, phosgene are poisonous gases prepared from chlorine gas.
  2. Because it forms blue coloured complex [Cu(NH3)4] +2 (aq)
    Reaction: Cu2+ + 4NH3 (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex}[Cu(NH3)4] +2 (aq)
 2
10.

Define the following terms with a suitable example of each:

  1. Polydentate ligand
  2. Homoleptic complex
Solution:
  1. A ligand several donor atoms. Example-EDTA
  2. A complex in which a metal is bound to only one kind of donor group / ligands. Example-[Co(NH3)6]3+
 2

OR

Using IUPAC norms, write the formulae for the following complexes:

  1. Potassium tri(oxalato)chromate(III)
  2. Hexaaquamanganese(II) sulphate
Solution:
  1. K3[Cr(ox)3]
  2. [Mn(H2O)6]SO4
 2
11.
    Although both [NiCl4]2- and [Ni(CO)4] have sp3 hybridisation yet [NiCl4]2- is paramagnetic and [Ni(CO)4] is diamagnetic. Give reason. (Atomic no. of Ni = 28)
  2. Write the electronic configuration of d5 on the basis of crystal field theory when
    1. {tex}\Delta {/tex}0 < P and
    2. {tex}\Delta {/tex}0 > P
Solution:
  1. In [NiCl4]2-, Cl- is a weak field ligand due to which there are two unpaired electrons in 3d orbital.
    In [Ni(CN)4]2-, CN- is a strong field ligand, it leads to pairing of electrons in 3d- orbital. So there are no unpaired electron in 3d- orbital.
    Hence, [NiCl4]2- is paramagnetic and [Ni(CO)4] is diamagnetic in nature.
  2. The electronic configuration of d5 on the basis of crystal field theory is 
    1. t2g3 eg2
    2. t2g5eg0
 2
12.

Write structures of main compounds A and B in each of the following reactions:

  1. {tex}\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{OH} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{PCC}} \mathrm{A} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{OH}(\mathrm{dry}\ \mathrm{HCl(g)}} \mathrm{B}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{COCH}_3 \xrightarrow{\mathrm{NaOI}} \mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B}{/tex}
Solution:
  1. A = CH3CHO, B = CH3CH(OH)OCH3
  2. A and B  = CHI3, C6H5COONa
 2

A + 2B {tex}\longrightarrow {/tex} C

1 0.2 0.3 4.2 {tex}\times {/tex} 10-2
2 0.1 0.1 6.0 {tex}\times {/tex} 10-3
3 0.4 0.3 1.68 {tex}\times {/tex} 10-1
4 0.1 0.4 2.40 {tex}\times {/tex} 10-2
  1. Find the order of reaction with respect to A and B.
  2. Write the rate law and overall order of reaction.
  3. Calculate the rate constant (k).
Solution:

Rate = k[A]p [B]q
On solving

  1. Order with respect to A = 2, B = 1
